sections.heading: Fast Facts on the 10-Year Lookback Rule
sections.content: The Act 60 rules for pre-move appreciation can be confusing, but the core concept is simple. If you sell an asset within 10 years of moving to Puerto Rico, the gain that happened before your move is taxed by the U.S. This is often called a 'built-in gain'. For example, if you bought a stock for $20 and it was worth $80 on your move date, that $60 of appreciation is the built-in gain. Only gains after the move date get the friendly Puerto Rican tax treatment. Waiting to sell for over 10 years can change this, but that requires long-term planning. Our platform is designed to quickly flag transactions that fall within this 10-year window, giving you an instant heads-up on potential U.S. tax liability. metaDescription: Don't get caught by depreciation recapture rules. Check your Act 60 compliance in minutes for Section 1245 & 1250 property. Fast, affordable, and instant.
h1: Quickly Check Your Act 60 Depreciation Recapture Exposure
heroIntro: Sold depreciable property? You might have a hidden tax liability. Depreciation recapture rules under Act 60 are complex, but checking for issues doesn't have to be. Use DecreeCheck to get a fast, affordable analysis of your potential recapture exposure on Section 1245 and 1250 assets in minutes, not weeks.

sections.heading: What is Depreciation Recapture and Why Does It Matter?
sections.content:
Depreciation lets you deduct the cost of an asset over time. But when you sell that asset for a profit, the government wants some of that tax benefit back. This is called "depreciation recapture." It means a portion of your gain, equal to the depreciation you claimed, is taxed as ordinary income, not as a lower-rate capital gain. For Act 60 holders, this is a critical distinction. The 0% capital gains tax rate you enjoy doesn't apply to recaptured depreciation. Ignoring this can lead to a surprise tax bill and penalties. Our platform is designed to quickly scan your sales and depreciation data to flag potential recapture amounts, giving you a clear picture of your tax situation instantly. The growth in AI-powered compliance tools shows that decree holders are tired of waiting weeks for answers from traditional firms.

Most business assets, from computers to machinery, fall under these rules. Even certain real estate transactions can trigger recapture. The problem is that many decree holders, especially those new to the island, overlook these rules. They assume all gains are covered by their decree. This is a dangerous and costly assumption. A quick check can save you thousands in the long run. Our tool is designed to give you that peace of mind without the high cost and long wait times associated with old-school CPA reviews, which can cost upwards of $5,000.

sections.heading: The Difference Between Section 1245 and 1250 Property
sections.content:
The two main categories you need to know are Section 1245 and Section 1250 property. Think of Section 1245 as your business equipment—things like vehicles, furniture, and machinery. When you sell these items, the recapture rule is strict: any gain up to the amount of depreciation you took is taxed as ordinary income. It's a full clawback. DecreeCheck's algorithm is designed to instantly identify these assets and calculate the potential recapture, so you know exactly where you stand.

Section 1250 property is real estate, like an office building or a rental unit. The rules here are a bit more relaxed but still tricky. Recapture on real estate generally only applies to depreciation taken in excess of the straight-line method. Since most property after 1986 uses straight-line, this is less common. However, there's a catch called "unrecaptured Section 1250 gain," which is taxed at a special 25% rate for U.S. purposes. You need to know how Puerto Rico treats this. Don't get bogged down in the details; let our tool do the heavy lifting and check your real estate sales for these hidden traps in minutes.

sections.heading: PR vs. US Rules: Don't Get Caught in the Middle
sections.content:
As an Act 60 resident, you're in a unique position, straddling both U.S. and Puerto Rico tax systems. This is where things get complicated. The asset's location and where the income is sourced determine which country's rules apply. Selling a U.S.-based asset might trigger U.S. recapture rules, even if you're a bona fide resident of Puerto Rico. Selling a PR-based asset falls under Hacienda's rules. The two are not identical. This complexity is a major reason why the GAO (GAO-26-107225) and the IRS are scrutinizing Act 60 returns.

Are you sure your tax preparer got the sourcing rules right? An error here could mean paying tax to the wrong government or, worse, not paying tax when it was due. DecreeCheck is designed to help identify these cross-jurisdictional conflicts. By uploading your information, you can get an instant check on how your asset sales are likely to be viewed by both the IRS and Hacienda. It’s a simple step to avoid a massive headache later.

metaDescription: How long can the IRS or Hacienda audit your Act 60 return? Check your compliance status in minutes and understand your audit risk period.
h1: Check Your Act 60 Statute of Limitations Window
heroIntro: Worried about how long tax authorities can look into your Act 60 filings? The statute of limitations isn't always straightforward. DecreeCheck helps you quickly understand your potential audit window from both the IRS and Hacienda so you can get peace of mind in minutes.

sections.heading: How Long is the Audit Window, Really?
sections.content:
The clock is ticking, but for how long? For Act 60 decree holders, there are two main timelines to watch. The IRS generally has three years to audit your federal return. But, if you've significantly understated your income (by more than 25%), that window extends to a lengthy six years. This is a common tripwire for Act 60 participants who misinterpret complex income sourcing rules. It’s a detail you can’t afford to miss.

Meanwhile, the Puerto Rico Treasury, or Hacienda, gives itself four years to review your local tax filings. This means for at least four years, you need to have your documentation in order. Juggling these different timelines can be confusing. DecreeCheck is designed to quickly analyze your filing situation and highlight your specific audit exposure period, removing the guesswork and giving you a clear, instant assessment of your risk.

sections.heading: What Can Extend the Statute of Limitations?
sections.content:
Think you're in the clear after three or four years? Not so fast. Certain actions, known as “tolling events,” can pause the statute of limitations, leaving your tax filings open to audit for much longer. The most obvious one is simply not filing a return—in that case, the clock never even starts. Filing a fraudulent return also gives tax authorities an indefinite amount of time to come after you.

For Act 60 individuals, a critical risk is being found not to be a “bona fide” resident of Puerto Rico. If the IRS makes this determination and you haven’t filed a U.S. tax return, the statute of limitations remains open indefinitely. It’s a serious risk that many decree holders overlook. Our system is designed to catch potential red flags related to residency and other tolling events, helping you understand if your audit window might be longer than you think.

h1: Act 60 vs. Act 20 & 22: What You Need to Know
heroIntro: Confused about Act 60, Act 20, and Act 22? You're not alone. Puerto Rico updated its tax laws, and we've got the simple breakdown. Learn the key differences in minutes and use our tool to quickly check if your return is on the right track.

sections.heading: The Big Switch: Why Act 60 Replaced Acts 20 & 22
paragraphs: Back in 2012, Puerto Rico rolled out two game-changing tax laws: Act 20 for businesses and Act 22 for investors. The goal was simple: bring money and people to the island. Act 20 offered a low 4% tax for service businesses, and Act 22 gave investors a 0% tax on their capital gains. It worked, but having two laws was a bit messy.

So, in 2020, the government combined them into one master plan: Act 60. Think of it as a software update. Act 20 and Act 22 didn't disappear; they just became chapters in the new, bigger Act 60 rulebook. This made things simpler and added a few new rules to make sure everyone was playing fair and contributing to the island.

sections.heading: What’s New in Act 60? The Fast Version
paragraphs: The new Act 60 came with a few important updates. If you're an investor (formerly Act 22), you now have to buy a house in Puerto Rico and donate $10,000 to local charities each year. If you have a business (formerly Act 20) making over $3 million a year, you need to hire at least one local employee.

These changes are about making sure the tax breaks benefit Puerto Rico as much as they benefit you. It’s a 'win-win' approach. The good news is that Act 60 also expanded the list of qualifying businesses to include modern industries like crypto and blockchain, so it’s not just about new rules, but also new opportunities.

sections.heading: Am I 'Grandfathered In'? Understanding Your Status
paragraphs: If you got your decree under Act 20 or 22 before 2020, you're likely 'grandfathered in.' This means you get to keep your original deal. But, you also have the choice to switch over to the new Act 60 rules if they work better for you. It's worth a quick check to see which is the better option.

Don't guess on your compliance. With the IRS increasing its focus on Act 60, a quick check can save you a lot of headaches later. Our tool is designed to give you a fast and affordable way to see where you stand. In just a few minutes, you can get the peace of mind that comes with knowing you're on top of your obligations.

faqItems.answer: Not on its own. Act 22 is now part of Act 60. If you're a new applicant, you'll be applying under Act 60 for the individual investor incentives.

faqItems.question: Do I have to buy a house now?
faqItems.answer: If you have an Act 60 decree as an individual investor, yes. You have two years from the date of your decree to purchase a primary residence in Puerto Rico.

title: Act 60 or FEIE? Get a Fast Answer on Your Tax Strategy
metaDescription: Confused between Act 60 and the FEIE? Check your eligibility and potential benefits in minutes. Get instant clarity on your best tax move.
h1: Act 60 vs. FEIE: Which Tax Incentive is Right for You?
heroIntro: Deciding between Puerto Rico's Act 60 and the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion can be complex. Our tool is designed to give you a quick, clear comparison based on your specific situation. Stop wondering and get the information you need to make a smart decision, fast.

sections.heading: Core Differences: Source of Income vs. Foreign Residency
sections.content: The fundamental distinction between Act 60 and the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) lies in the type of income they target and the residency status required. Act 60, specifically the Export Services incentive, offers a 4% corporate tax rate on income earned from services performed within Puerto Rico for clients outside of Puerto Rico. This makes it a powerful tool for service-based businesses and entrepreneurs who become bona fide residents of the island. The tax benefit is tied to income sourced *from* Puerto Rico. In contrast, the FEIE, governed by IRC Section 911, allows U.S. citizens or residents living abroad to exclude a certain amount of their *foreign-sourced* earned income from U.S. federal taxation. This exclusion is not tied to a specific jurisdiction like Puerto Rico but rather to the taxpayer's presence in any foreign country. A recent Government Accountability Office report (GAO-26-107225) highlighted the importance of correctly sourcing income, a key compliance area for both programs that automated review platforms are designed to verify.

sections.heading: Eligibility and Residency Requirements: A Detailed Breakdown
sections.content: Qualifying for these powerful tax incentives requires meeting stringent and distinct residency tests. To be considered a bona fide resident of Puerto Rico for Act 60 purposes, an individual must satisfy three tests annually: the Presence Test (spending at least 183 days in Puerto Rico), the Tax Home Test (maintaining their primary place of business or employment in Puerto Rico), and the Closer Connection Test (having stronger personal and economic ties to Puerto Rico than to the U.S. or any other country). These rules are notoriously complex and a primary focus of IRS audits. The FEIE, on the other hand, requires a U.S. citizen to meet either the Physical Presence Test (being physically present in a foreign country or countries for 330 full days out of any 12-month period) or the Bona Fide Residence Test (being a resident of a foreign country for an uninterrupted period that includes an entire tax year). While seemingly more straightforward, documenting one's foreign residency for the FEIE can still be a significant compliance burden. The high failure rate in CPA-led reviews, often exceeding initial client expectations, underscores the difficulty of navigating these requirements without specialized tools.

sections.heading: Benefit Comparison: Tax Rates and Income Types
sections.content: The financial advantages of Act 60 and the FEIE differ significantly in their application and scope. Act 60's Export Services incentive provides a low 4% corporate tax rate on eligible business profits and 100% tax exemption on dividend distributions to Puerto Rican residents. Furthermore, the Individual Resident Investor incentive offers a 100% exemption from Puerto Rico taxes on all passive income, including interest, dividends, and, most notably, all capital gains accrued after establishing residency. The FEIE, in contrast, provides an exclusion for *earned income* only, up to an annual limit that is indexed for inflation ($120,000 for tax year 2023). It does not apply to passive income, capital gains, or dividends. Therefore, for an active trader, venture capitalist, or consultant with significant business and investment income, Act 60 can offer a far more comprehensive tax shield. Traditional CPA firms often charge between $5,000 and $25,000 for a review to model these differences, a cost that is being dramatically reduced by the adoption of AI-powered compliance platforms.

sections.heading: Strategic Choice: Which Path Maximizes Your Tax Advantage?
sections.content: Choosing between Act 60 and the FEIE is a critical strategic decision that depends entirely on your income profile, business structure, and long-term financial goals. An entrepreneur with a U.S.-based consulting firm who can relocate their operations and residency to Puerto Rico would likely find Act 60 far more advantageous due to the 4% corporate rate and 0% tax on capital gains. Conversely, a U.S. citizen working as an employee for a multinational corporation in Dubai might find the FEIE a simpler and more direct way to reduce their U.S. tax burden, as they have no Puerto Rico-sourced income. The decision is not always clear-cut, especially for individuals with mixed income sources or complex international business structures. The digitally underserved nature of the Act 60 compliance market means many decree holders rely on anecdotal advice rather than rigorous analysis. Leveraging a comprehensive review tool can help identify the optimal structure and ensure it is properly documented to withstand potential scrutiny from tax authorities like the IRS or Puerto Rico's Department of Treasury (Hacienda).

faqItems.question: Can I claim both Act 60 benefits and the FEIE?
faqItems.answer: No, you generally cannot claim both for the same income. Income sourced from Puerto Rico under Act 60 rules is not considered 'foreign earned income' for the purposes of the FEIE. Attempting to do so is a major compliance red flag. Our review process is designed to catch these potential issues before you file.

faqItems.question: What are the biggest mistakes people make when choosing between Act 60 and FEIE?
faqItems.answer: The most common errors are misinterpreting the complex income sourcing rules and failing to meet the strict bona fide residency requirements for Puerto Rico. Another significant mistake is not documenting the choice and business structure adequately, which can lead to challenges during an audit. A thorough, automated review can help identify these weaknesses.

faqItems.question: Is the FEIE or Act 60 better for crypto traders?
faqItems.answer: For most active crypto traders, Act 60 is significantly more advantageous. The FEIE only applies to *earned income* and does not cover capital gains from trading digital assets. Under Act 60, capital gains accrued after becoming a bona fide resident of Puerto Rico can be 100% tax-exempt. This is a critical distinction that can result in millions of dollars in tax savings.

faqItems.question: How does the IRS view Act 60 compared to the FEIE?
faqItems.answer: The IRS is aware of and scrutinizes both programs. The IRS launched a specific campaign (Campaign 685) to audit high-income individuals claiming Act 60 benefits, focusing on residency and income sourcing. While the FEIE is a long-standing part of the tax code, it is also subject to audit. Proper compliance and documentation are critical for defending your position under either regime.

sections.heading: Essential Forms & Dates You Can't Miss
sections.content: Compliance starts with the right forms at the right time. You'll need to file Form 482 in Puerto Rico and Form 1040 in the US, making sure to claim the IRC Section 933 exclusion. The April 15th deadline is critical. On top of that, your DDEC annual report is due by May 31st. It's a simple list, but missing any of these can cause major headaches. Our system gives you a rapid check against these core requirements, so you know where you stand instantly. No more digging through government websites; get the essential facts in seconds.

sections.heading: Quick Residency Verification: Are You Covered?
sections.content: Your bona fide residency is the foundation of your decree. You need to pass the presence test (183+ days), the tax home test, and the closer connection test. What does that mean in practice? It means having a PR driver's license, local bank accounts, and proof you actually live on the island. The IRS is actively scrutinizing this, so a quick check is essential. Our tool lets you quickly self-assess your residency status against the key requirements, highlighting potential gaps before they become problems. It's a fast and easy way to gauge your audit risk without paying for a multi-thousand dollar review.

sections.heading: Income Reporting Basics for Act 60
sections.content: Getting the 0-4% tax rates depends entirely on how you source your income. Did you earn it in Puerto Rico? For export services, that means the work was done here. For capital gains, it means you realized the gain as a PR resident. It sounds simple, but mistakes are common. You also have to keep filing US forms for foreign assets, like the FBAR. DecreeCheck quickly scans for common income sourcing and asset reporting errors, giving you a fast and affordable second opinion on your return. Why wait for an expert when you can check the basics yourself in minutes?

title: Act 60 Transfer Pricing Check: Fast & Easy
metaDescription: Worried about transfer pricing? Get a quick check of your Act 60 compliance. Fast, affordable, and built for busy entrepreneurs.
h1: Quick Guide to Act 60 Transfer Pricing Rules
heroIntro: Don't let transfer pricing rules slow you down. Our tool helps you check your compliance in minutes, giving you peace of mind without the high cost of traditional consultants.

sections.heading: What is Transfer Pricing?
sections.content: Transfer pricing is about how you price transactions between your Act 60 business in Puerto Rico and any related companies you have in the U.S. or elsewhere. Think of it as a set of rules to make sure the prices are fair, as if the companies were unrelated. The IRS wants to see that you're not just moving money around to avoid taxes. For example, if your U.S. company provides services to your Puerto Rico company, you need to charge a fair market price for those services. Getting this right is a key part of staying compliant with your Act 60 decree.

sections.heading: Why It Matters for Your Act 60 Business
sections.content: The IRS is paying close attention to Act 60 businesses, and transfer pricing is one of their hot topics. If they think your pricing isn't fair, they can adjust your income and hit you with back taxes and penalties. This could wipe out the tax savings you're getting from Act 60. That's why it's so important to have a handle on your transfer pricing. It's not just about following the rules; it's about protecting your bottom line and the benefits of your decree. A little bit of planning and documentation can save you a lot of headaches down the road.

sections.heading: Staying Compliant: The Arm's Length Principle
sections.content: The main rule in transfer pricing is the "arm's length principle." This just means that the price for a transaction between your related companies should be the same as if they were two unrelated companies. There are a few different ways to figure out what that price should be, like comparing it to similar transactions on the open market. The key is to pick a method that makes sense for your business and to document why you chose it. This documentation is your proof that you're playing by the rules and can be a lifesaver if the IRS comes knocking.

faqItems.question: Do I really need to worry about transfer pricing?
faqItems.answer: Yes, if your Act 60 business has transactions with related companies, you definitely need to be aware of transfer pricing rules. The IRS is actively looking at this issue.

faqItems.answer: The easiest way is to be proactive. Document your transactions and the reasons for your pricing. Our DecreeCheck tool can help you spot potential issues quickly and easily.

h1: Act 60 Estimated Tax Payments Made Simple
heroIntro: Confused about estimated taxes under Act 60? You're not alone. Our tool simplifies the process, letting you check your compliance in minutes. Get the clarity you need without the high cost of traditional advisors.

sections.heading: Do You Need to Pay Estimated Taxes?
sections.content: If you have an Act 60 decree, you might think you're off the hook for taxes. But that's not always the case. If you have income that isn't subject to withholding, like from a business or investments, you may need to make estimated tax payments. Generally, if you expect to owe more than $1,000 in taxes for the year, you need to pay estimated taxes. Our quick check can help you figure out if this applies to you. We make it easy to understand your obligations so you can avoid any surprises at tax time.

sections.heading: Quarterly Deadlines You Can't Miss
sections.content: The government wants its money throughout the year, not all at once. That's why there are quarterly deadlines for estimated tax payments: April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15. Missing these dates can lead to penalties, which can add up quickly. Our tool helps you stay on top of these deadlines. We send you reminders and help you calculate how much you need to pay each quarter. It's a simple way to stay compliant and avoid unnecessary fees.

sections.heading: The Safe Harbor Rule: Your Safety Net
sections.content: Worried about underpaying your estimated taxes? The safe harbor rule is your friend. It's a way to avoid penalties, even if you end up owing more than you paid. The basic idea is that if you pay either 90% of what you owe for the current year or 100% of what you owed last year (110% if you're a high-income earner), you're generally in the clear. Our tool can help you see if you qualify for the safe harbor, giving you one less thing to worry about. It's a simple check that can save you a lot of money and stress.

faqItems.question: How do I know how much to pay in estimated taxes?
faqItems.answer: Our tool can help you with that. We'll ask you a few simple questions about your income and then give you an estimate of what you should pay each quarter. It's a lot easier than trying to figure it out on your own.

faqItems.question: What if my income changes during the year?

sections.heading: Capital Loss Carryforward Basics
sections.content: Under Act 60, if you have capital losses, you can use them to offset your capital gains. This is called a capital loss carryforward. In Puerto Rico, you can carry forward these losses for up to seven years. However, you can't use your losses to wipe out all of your gains. There's a rule that limits the use of capital losses to 90% of your capital gains for the year. This means you'll still have to pay tax on 10% of your gains, even if you have enough losses to cover them all. Our tool can help you calculate how this rule affects you.

sections.heading: How Act 60 and PR Tax Rules Work Together
sections.content: Act 60 doesn't replace Puerto Rico's entire tax system. It works with it. This is important when it comes to capital losses. The type of loss you have and where it came from matters. For example, a loss from selling stocks is treated differently than a loss from selling real estate. Our tool is designed to help you sort through these rules and see how they apply to your specific situation. We make it easy to understand the interaction between Act 60 and the regular Puerto Rico tax rules, so you can be confident in your tax filings.

sections.heading: Quick Tips for Tax-Loss Harvesting
sections.content: Tax-loss harvesting is a simple way to lower your tax bill. It just means selling investments that have lost value to offset gains from your winning investments. If you're an Act 60 beneficiary, this can be a smart move. But you need to be careful about the wash-sale rule. This rule says you can't claim a loss if you buy the same or a very similar investment within 30 days. Our tool can help you keep track of your trades and avoid this common mistake. We provide clear, actionable tips to help you make the most of tax-loss harvesting.

faqItems.question: Can I use old losses to offset my gains now?
faqItems.answer: It depends. If the losses are from before you moved to Puerto Rico, you probably can't use them to offset your Puerto Rico gains. It's best to check with a tax professional to be sure.

faqItems.question: What's the 90% rule again?
faqItems.answer: The 90% rule means you can only use capital losses to offset up to 90% of your capital gains. So, if you have $10,000 in gains, you can only use losses to offset $9,000 of them. You'll still have to pay tax on the remaining $1,000.

faqItems.question: What records do I need to keep?
faqItems.answer: You should keep all your brokerage statements and trade confirmations. These documents prove your gains and losses. If you get audited, you'll need them to back up your tax return.

h1: Act 60 Withholding, Made Simple
heroIntro: Don't let withholding rules slow you down. Act 60 has specific requirements for paying service providers in Puerto Rico. Check your compliance in minutes and get back to business.

sections.heading: What is Withholding Tax in PR?
sections.content: When you pay someone for services performed in Puerto Rico, you often need to hold back a portion of that payment for taxes. This is called withholding. The money you withhold is sent to the Puerto Rico government. This applies to many Act 60 businesses. The basic idea is to make sure taxes get paid on income earned in Puerto Rico. The standard rate for many professional services is 10%, but it can be different depending on who you're paying and what they're doing. Getting this right is a key part of staying compliant with your decree. It’s a simple concept, but the details matter. Our tool helps you quickly check if you are on the right track with your payments.

sections.heading: Who Do I Need to Withhold For?
sections.content: You generally need to withhold taxes on payments for services performed in Puerto Rico. This includes payments to individuals, partnerships, and corporations. A common example is paying a local consultant, for which you would likely withhold 10%. The rules can be different if you are paying a company that isn't based in Puerto Rico. For those, the rate can be much higher, sometimes 29%. It’s important to know who you are paying and where they are based. You can't assume the same rule applies to everyone. Keeping clear records of your service providers and the payments you make is essential. This helps you apply the correct withholding and report it accurately at the end of the year.

sections.heading: Reporting Your Withholding
sections.content: Withholding the tax is only the first step. You also have to report these payments and the withheld amounts to the Puerto Rico Treasury Department, known as Hacienda. This is typically done using a series of forms, most commonly the Form 480 series. These forms detail who you paid, how much you paid them, and how much tax you withheld. Filing these forms correctly and on time is just as important as the withholding itself. Missing these deadlines or filing incorrect information can lead to penalties. The process is straightforward once you understand the requirements. It’s about being organized and making sure your records match what you report to the government.

faqItems.question: Is there a simple withholding rate for everything?
faqItems.answer: No, there isn't one single rate. It depends on the service provider and the type of service. A 10% rate is common for resident professionals, but it can be 29% for some non-resident companies. It's best to check the specific rate for each payment.

faqItems.question: Do I need to withhold for services performed outside of PR?
faqItems.answer: Generally, no. The withholding requirement applies to income from sources within Puerto Rico. If the service is performed entirely outside of Puerto Rico, withholding is typically not required, but sourcing rules can be complex.

faqItems.question: What is a Form 480.6?
faqItems.answer: Form 480.6 is an informational return used in Puerto Rico to report payments made to individuals and companies for services, among other things. It's similar to a Form 1099 in the U.S. You must file one for each provider you paid during the year.

title: Austin to Puerto Rico Tax Move: Quick Check
metaDescription: Moving from Austin to Puerto Rico? Check your Act 60 tax compliance in minutes. Fast, affordable, and built for the modern entrepreneur.
h1: Austin to Puerto Rico: Check Your Act 60 Tax Savings
heroIntro: Thinking of swapping Austin for Puerto Rico? It's a big move with big tax implications. Our tool is designed to give you a quick and easy check of your potential Act 60 compliance. Get the clarity you need in minutes, not weeks.

sections.heading: From No State Tax to No Federal Tax
sections.content: Leaving Austin for Puerto Rico means shifting your focus from state to federal taxes. In Texas, you pay no state income tax, but you still owe the IRS. In Puerto Rico, under Act 60, you can potentially pay 0% in federal income tax on income you earn on the island. This is the main attraction of the move. To get this benefit, you have to be a 'bona fide resident' of Puerto Rico and your income must be from a Puerto Rican source. It sounds simple, but the rules can be tricky. Our check is designed to quickly run through the basic requirements and flag areas you need to pay closer attention to. It's a fast first step to understanding if this move makes sense for you financially.

sections.heading: Are You a Bona Fide Resident?
sections.content: To get Act 60 benefits, you have to prove to the IRS that you are a bona fide resident of Puerto Rico. This means more than just being there for 183 days a year. You also need to have your main workplace and your closest personal and financial ties in Puerto Rico. Think of it as a three-part test: the presence test (183+ days), the tax home test (your work is in PR), and the closer connection test (your life is in PR). Our tool helps you do a quick self-assessment against these tests. It’s a simple way to see if you’re on the right track to establishing bona fide residency and securing your tax benefits.

sections.heading: Is Your Income from a Puerto Rico Source?
sections.content: This is a big one. Only income from a Puerto Rico source gets the 0% tax rate. If you work remotely for a U.S. company while living in Puerto Rico, you need to be careful. The IRS will look at where your work is actually being done and who it benefits. Just because you are on the island doesn't automatically make your salary PR-sourced income. It’s important to understand this distinction to avoid any surprises from the IRS. Our check can help you think through how your income might be classified and what you need to do to make sure it qualifies for Act 60.

faqItems.question: Is it hard to become a bona fide resident of Puerto Rico?
faqItems.answer: It requires careful planning. You need to meet three tests: the Presence Test, the Tax Home Test, and the Closer Connection Test. Our tool can help you quickly assess where you stand on these requirements.

faqItems.question: What's the biggest mistake people make when moving to Puerto Rico for tax reasons?
faqItems.answer: A common mistake is assuming all their income is automatically tax-free. You need to make sure your income is properly sourced to Puerto Rico. It's a critical step that many people overlook.

faqItems.question: How is this different from just living in a no-tax state like Texas?
faqItems.answer: In Texas, you don't pay state income tax, but you still pay federal income tax. In Puerto Rico, with Act 60, you can potentially eliminate your federal income tax on income earned on the island. It's a much bigger potential tax saving.

title: Act 60 for Attorneys: Fast Tax Compliance Check
metaDescription: Quickly check your Act 60 compliance. Our tool helps attorneys understand legal service income sourcing and bar admission rules.
h1: Act 60 for Attorneys: Check Your Compliance in Minutes
heroIntro: Don’t guess on your Act 60 compliance. Our fast and easy-to-use tool helps you check your legal service income sourcing and understand the rules for practicing law in Puerto Rico. Get the clarity you need in minutes.

sections.heading: What is Act 60 for Attorneys?
sections.content: Act 60 is a tax incentive program in Puerto Rico that can be very beneficial for attorneys. It offers a low 4% tax rate on income from legal services provided to clients outside of Puerto Rico. This means if you have a law practice with clients in the U.S. or other countries, you could save a lot on taxes. But, you have to follow the rules. The main one is that your services have to be considered 'export services,' which means you are doing the work in Puerto Rico for clients who are not in Puerto Rico. Our tool can help you quickly see if your services fit this description.

sections.heading: Sourcing Your Income Correctly
sections.content: One of the most important parts of Act 60 is making sure you are sourcing your income correctly. This means you have to prove that you are doing the work in Puerto Rico. For lawyers, this can be tricky if you travel a lot or work with people in other places. You need to keep good records to show where you were when you were working. The IRS is paying close attention to this, so it’s important to get it right. Our tool can help you understand what kind of records you need to keep.

sections.heading: Practicing Law in Puerto Rico
sections.content: If you want to move to Puerto Rico and take advantage of Act 60, you also need to think about whether you need to be a member of the Puerto Rico bar. If you are giving legal advice to clients in Puerto Rico, you will generally need to be admitted to the local bar. This can be a long process, so it’s something to plan for. If you are only providing services to clients outside of Puerto Rico, you may not need to be a member of the local bar. Our tool can help you understand the rules so you can make the right decision for your practice.

faqItems.question: Do I need to be a member of the Puerto Rico bar to use Act 60?
faqItems.answer: It depends on who your clients are. If you are advising clients in Puerto Rico, you will likely need to be a member of the local bar. If your clients are all outside of Puerto Rico, you may not need to be. It’s a good idea to check with a legal expert to be sure.

faqItems.question: What kind of records do I need to keep?
faqItems.answer: You should keep records that show where you were when you were working. This could include things like timesheets, travel receipts, and notes from your project management software. The more detailed your records, the better.
